lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market screen printers repeatedly serving businesses, institutions, brands, distributors, teams, schools, events, and promotional-product customers on apparel, textiles, labels, signs, stationery, invitations, components, and other purchased substrates.
lens.excluded_activities: Publishers, book printers, fabric grey-goods mills, product manufacturers whose printing is captive, pure brokers without transferable operations, hobby shops, non-control financing vehicles, and owner-dependent art practices without recurring external customer relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Job-order and recurring-program revenue priced per design, setup, color, item, run, or fulfillment program, often including artwork, screen preparation, printing, curing, finishing, packing, online storefront, and distribution services.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation structure for the broader NAICS 323 printing and related support subsector, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in screen printing. In May 2023, printing workers were 36.61% of NAICS 323 employment, including press operators at 23.98%, prepress at 4.05%, and binding/finishing at 8.58%; graphic design, sales, management, business, and office roles add document- and communication-heavy work. Screen printing can have more manual setup, garment handling, ink work, curing, and packing than other print methods, so applying the broader mix likely overstates exposure unless offset by small-shop quoting, art, and customer-service intensity.
a.rationale: AI can substitute portions of artwork cleanup and variation, specification capture, quoting, mockups, proof correspondence, order entry, scheduling, purchasing, online-store support, and administration. Screen coating, exposure, registration, ink mixing, press setup, loading, printing, curing, reclaiming, finishing, packing, and physical quality remain hands-on.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-time study across 20 screen printers segmented by apparel, promotional, industrial, label, and signage work, including current workflow software and direct labor by step, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: BLS evidence covers all printing, not 323113.
Occupation shares do not directly measure task exposure or existing automation.

rho.rationale: Artwork, quote, proof, order, scheduling, and customer-service workflows are already digital enough for assisted automation within five years. Highly variable source files, subjective color and placement, small-firm data quality, legacy shop systems, customer approvals, and physical production exceptions limit implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after deployments showing sustained hours saved, quote turnaround, art and proof revisions, order-entry errors, schedule adherence, remake rates, exception rates, and user adoption across representative screen printers would materially update the range.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed adoption probability.
Digital-native web-store operators may implement faster than relationship-led manual shops.

e.rationale: Screen printing is generally external job-order work and recurring programs are common, but the broad establishment base includes many microbusinesses, owner-artisan shops, captive decorators, distressed shops, and firms outside the actual EBITDA band or without transferable customer relationships.
e.change_evidence: A verified list of the 80 dataset-estimated LMM candidates with ownership, recurring revenue, product mix, normalized EBITDA, owner dependence, equipment, and customer concentration would replace this judgment.
caveats: Census reports 5,854 employer establishments in 2023, far broader than the supplied LMM firm estimate.
The provided n uses a paper-industry margin bridge that may not reflect screen-printing economics.

s5.rationale: A fragmented owner-operated industry should generate retirement and strategic tuck-in sales, especially where customers, staff, equipment, and online programs transfer. However, many shops close, sell only equipment or customer lists, transfer internally, or prove too owner-dependent for a qualifying control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched ten-year set of screen-printing control deals from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and sector advisers, paired with owner-age and succession-plan data for the eligible universe, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No comprehensive six-digit control-transfer series was found.
Asset sales and customer-book purchases without a transferable operating firm must be excluded.

q.rationale: Custom designs, setup economics, rapid turns, color and placement expertise, online storefronts, fulfillment, and account service reduce direct price comparability and can protect gains. Competitive local quotes, promotional distributors, print-on-demand platforms, large-program rebids, and low switching costs for simple apparel jobs pass some recurring savings to customers.
q.change_evidence: Representative contracts, quote histories, renewal cadence, program bids, customer concentration, price-change records, win/loss reasons, and margin bridges following workflow automation would establish actual retention.
caveats: No source directly measures contractual sharing of AI-derived benefit.
Capture differs sharply between industrial/specification work and commodity T-shirt programs.

d5.rationale: Decorated apparel, branded promotional items, teams, schools, events, labels, and industrial markings retain physical use cases, and short-run customization supports volume. Economic cyclicality, fashion and event volatility, digital marketing substitution, direct-to-garment and transfer technologies, reuse, imports, and customer insourcing prevent a confident growth assumption.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census shipment data and physical impressions by screen-printed product, distributor and apparel-decoration order volumes, event and team-program cohorts, and customer substitution data would materially change the range.
caveats: No complete constant-price, constant-quality forecast exists for 323113.
BLS occupational decline reflects productivity and technology substitution as well as demand.

o.rationale: Remaining demand is for a physical image or functional marking on a substrate and therefore requires accountable artwork preparation, ink and process choice, registration, printing or a substitute decoration process, curing, quality, and fulfillment. Customers can self-design online and some can insource transfers or small equipment, but software alone cannot deliver the finished physical item.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and lost-order codes quantifying in-house decoration, print-on-demand platforms, direct-to-garment substitution, transfer application, offshore sourcing, and software-only elimination would materially change this value.
caveats: Alternative physical decoration processes may displace screen printers even when decorated-product demand persists.
Environmental controls and ink changes reinforce operator accountability but do not guarantee domestic demand.

narrative.executive_view: Commercial screen printing combines labor-intensive physical production with a meaningful digital front end in artwork, quoting, proofs, order management, and customer service. AI can improve the front end, while the screen room, press, curing, reclaiming, finishing, and fulfillment remain hands-on and demand varies with apparel, promotion, event, label, and industrial end markets.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can clean and adapt artwork, generate mockups, compare specifications, assist quotes, summarize proof changes, ingest orders, schedule jobs, triage exceptions, and support customers and online stores. Physical screen preparation, registration, ink handling, printing, curing, cleaning, packing, and final quality remain operator tasks.
narrative.value_capture: Customization, turnaround, consistent placement and color, program administration, and fulfillment create service value that can protect savings. Simple apparel and promotional work is readily quoted across local shops and online platforms, so price competition will share away part of recurring gains.
narrative.firm_availability: Census reports thousands of employer establishments, but the supplied dataset estimates only 80 firms in the target economic band, highlighting the industry's microbusiness skew and the uncertainty in the margin bridge. Transferability depends on recurring programs, staff, equipment, customer diversification, and whether art and sales relationships reside beyond the owner.
narrative.demand_durability: Physical decorated apparel, promotional goods, labels, signs, and industrial markings should persist, but channel and technology can change. Direct-to-garment, transfers, online self-service, imports, and in-house equipment can displace conventional screen operators even without eliminating the decorated product.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest unknowns are the six-digit labor mix, true LMM population, owner dependence, product and channel mix, current workflow software, customer concentration, and substitution among screen, digital, transfer, and offshore production. Ink and solvent controls add implementation constraints, while the supplied inputs mix vintages and apply a margin bridge.
narrative.principal_driver: High-frequency artwork, quote, proof, order, scheduling, and customer workflows sit beside a relatively high labor-to-receipts base.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most shop labor remains physical, and easy-to-quote apparel work faces strong local, online, alternative-process, and offshore competition.

sources.title: Printing and Related Support Activities - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: BLS reports 378,410 workers in NAICS 323 in May 2023; printing workers were 138,520 (36.61%), including 90,740 press operators (23.98%), 15,330 prepress workers (4.05%), and 32,450 binding/finishing workers (8.58%).

sources.title: 323113: Commercial Screen Printing
sources.supports: Census defines job-order screen printing on purchased stock and explicitly includes apparel and textile products such as T-shirts, caps, jackets, towels, and napkins; it reports 5,854 employer establishments in 2023.

sources.title: Monitoring Information by Industry - Printing and Publishing
sources.supports: EPA states that printing emissions arise primarily from evaporation of organic solvents in inks, identifies press, drier, ink, cleaning, and mixing emission points, and notes use of thermal and catalytic oxidizers on screen-printing drier exhausts for solvent-borne inks.

sources.title: Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics
sources.supports: BLS projects printing press operator employment to decline from 150,200 in 2024 to 138,000 in 2034, an 8.1% decline, and print binding/finishing employment to decline 16.1%; these are broad occupation projections, not a screen-print demand forecast.
